text: Transition elements are defined as elements that have:
options: Partially filled d orbitals in ground state or common oxidation state
explanation: Transition elements: partially filled d orbitals in ground state OR any common oxidation state. Zn, Cd, Hg are border cases.
name: Definition of Transition Elements

text: The general electronic configuration of transition elements (3d series) is:
explanation: 3d transition series: [Ar] 3d¹⁻¹⁰ 4s¹⁻². Exceptions: Cr ([Ar]3d⁵4s¹) and Cu ([Ar]3d¹⁰4s¹) due to extra stability of half-filled/fully-filled d.
name: Electronic Config of Transition Elements

text: Variable oxidation states in transition elements are due to:
options: Small energy difference between 3d and 4s orbitals allowing multiple electron losses
explanation: 3d and 4s orbitals have similar energies, so various numbers of electrons can be lost. E.g., Mn shows +2 to +7 oxidation states.
name: Variable Oxidation States

text: The colour of transition metal compounds is due to:
options: d-d electronic transitions in the presence of ligands
explanation: Colour: when white light hits a complex, specific wavelengths are absorbed for d-d transitions (electrons promoted between d levels split by ligand field). Complementary colour is observed.
name: Colour of Transition Compounds

text: The highest oxidation state shown by Mn is:
explanation: Mn can show +7 in KMnO₄ (potassium permanganate). Mn: [Ar]3d⁵4s² → can lose all 7 valence electrons.
name: Highest Oxidation State of Mn

text: KMnO₄ in acidic medium acts as:
explanation: In acidic medium: \( \text{MnO}_4^- + 8\text{H}^+ + 5e^- \to \text{Mn}^{2+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \). KMnO₄ is a powerful oxidant. Mn: +7 → +2.
name: KMnO₄ in Acidic Medium

text: K₂Cr₂O₇ in acidic medium: the half reaction at cathode involves Cr going from:
name: K₂Cr₂O₇ Reduction

text: Which transition metal has the highest melting point?
explanation: Tungsten (W) has the highest melting point (3422°C) of all metals, due to strong metallic bonding (d-electron contribution).
name: Highest Melting Point Metal

options: Decrease in atomic radius across f-block due to poor shielding by f electrons
Increase in atomic radius in lanthanides
explanation: Lanthanide contraction: steady decrease in atomic/ionic radius across lanthanides due to poor shielding of 4f electrons. Causes 5d and 4d elements to have similar sizes.
name: Lanthanide Contraction

text: Catalytic properties of transition metals are due to:
options: Variable oxidation states and ability to adsorb reactants
explanation: Transition metals are good catalysts because: (1) variable oxidation states allow intermediate formation, (2) d-orbitals adsorb and activate reactants.
name: Catalytic Properties

text: The most stable oxidation state of iron (Fe) is:
explanation: Fe shows +2 (Fe²⁺, ferrous) and +3 (Fe³⁺, ferric). Fe³⁺ ([Ar]3d⁵) is particularly stable (half-filled d). Both are common and stable.
name: Stable Oxidation States of Fe

text: Which element among 3d series has maximum number of unpaired electrons?
explanation: Mn: [Ar]3d⁵4s². 3d⁵ has 5 unpaired electrons (Hund's rule). Maximum for 3d series is 5 (at Mn, d⁵).
name: Maximum Unpaired Electrons

text: The magnetic moment formula for transition metal ions is:
explanation: Magnetic moment: \( \mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)} \) Bohr Magnetons (BM), where n = number of unpaired electrons.
name: Magnetic Moment Formula

text: The f-block elements (lanthanides) have the electron being filled in:
explanation: Lanthanides (Ce to Lu): filling of 4f orbitals. Actinides: filling of 5f orbitals. Both are inner transition elements.
name: f-Block Orbital Filling

text: The process used to extract iron in a blast furnace uses which reducing agent?
explanation: In blast furnace: C burns to form CO, which reduces iron oxide: \( \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \to 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2 \). Both C and CO act as reducing agents.
name: Blast Furnace Reducing Agent
